title: Go HTTP Upload and Download Gateways · Execution Trace: Streaming Response Commit
summary: A Go handler buffers multipart forms and S3 objects in memory, trusts proxy headers, writes status too early, and continues transferring after the client leaves. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the streaming response commit increment in streaming Go file-gateway handler. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Delay response commitment until safe, set representation headers, stream bounded data, and record partial-write terminal states.
Use MaxBytesReader or bounded readers and streaming multipart iteration without ParseMultipartForm disk or memory surprises.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Streaming Response Commit · Theory Streaming Response Commit
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 5089 during a compliance restore drill. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate a streaming Go file handler on request admission and goroutine cleanup; do not treat an ETag, CORS result, emulator, or successful SDK call as stronger proof than it is; then change the object version and prove stale authority is rejected. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Delay response commitment until safe, set representation headers, stream bounded data, and record partial-write terminal states.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Streaming Response Commit contract for the supplied data.
